coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot induces tolerance to arsenic by regulating arsenic uptake, reactive oxygen species detoxification and defense-related gene expression in Cicer arietinum L
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Chandrakar, Vibhuti
Yadu, Bhumika
Korram, Jyoti
Satnami, Manmohan L.
Dubey, Amit
Kumar, Meetul
Keshavkant, S.
coredata.dc:description: The scientific and technological applications of one of the nanomaterials viz.; carbon dot (C-dots), having extraordinary properties, is becoming an emerging and ongoing research area in recent times. In the present study, we have evaluated the effectiveness of C-dots in reducing arsenic (As) toxicity by analyzing physiological, biochemical and molecular parameters in Cicer arietinum L. The results revealed that As decreased the germination rate, growth, biomass, and membrane stability of the cell to a significant extent. Further, As was taken up by the growing seeds which eventually caused cell death. Levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), stress markers (malondialdehyde), activities of defensive enzymes (glutathione-S-transferase and pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthetase) and non-enzymatic antioxidant contents (proline and glutathione) were increased under As stress. Moreover, As treatment resulted in the up-regulation of expressions of NADPH oxidase and defense-related genes in Cicer arietinum L. However, application of C-dots along with As improved the germination and growth of Cicer arietinum L. Exogenous application of C-dots, enhanced the expressions of defense-related genes and, contents of proline and glutathione, thereby causing considerable reductions in ROS, and malondialdehyde levels. Overall, this study suggests the possible involvement of C-dots in lowering the toxic effects of As on biomass by reducing As uptake and, inducing the activities/gene expressions and contents of enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Arsenic
Carbon dot
Cicer arietinum L.
Gene expression
Nanomaterials
Oxidative damage
Reactive oxygen species

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dot
Membrane stability index and lipid peroxidation
Activities and gene expressions of NADPH oxidase, glutathione-S-transferase and pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthetase

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2020 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Chhattisgarh Council of Science and Technology
Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Vis absorption and PL spectra of carbon dot (C-dots). Inset: C-dots solution under visible light (left) and 365 nm UV light (right), (B) The TEM image of C-dots, and (C) The FTIR spectra.

Comparison of FTIR spectra of (a) control, (b) carbon dot, (c) arsenic, and (d) arsenic + carbon dot treated Cicer arietinum L. in the mid-infrared region (4000-400 cm−1). Characteristic functional groups contributing to the formation of bands at specific wave-numbers are indicated in the form of peaks.

Correlation graphs plotted between (a) germination percentage, dry mass and arsenic accrual; (b) radicle length, membrane stability index and arsenic accrual; (c) superoxide, hydrogen peroxide and arsenic accrual; (d) proline, glutathione and membrane stability index; and (e) superoxide and NADPH oxidase.

Effects of carbon dot (C-dots) on (a) cell death, (b) superoxide, (c) hydrogen peroxide, and (d) loss of membrane integrity/lipid peroxidation visualized as blue, purple, brown and pink coloration, respectively, in Cicer arietinum L. radicles under arsenic (As) stress. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Fluorescence visualization of (a) superoxide, and (b) hydrogen peroxide in Cicer arietinum L. radicles treated with arsenic (As) and carbon dot (C-dots).

Alterations in the activities of (a) NADPH oxidase (NOX), (b) glutathione-S-transferase (GST), (c) pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthetase (P5CS), and (d) expression of these genes in Cicer arietinum L. radicles treated with arsenic (As) and carbon dot (C-dots). Each bar represents mean ± SE of five replicates. Different alphabets indicate significant differences (P < 0.05).

Effects of arsenic (As) and/or carbon dot (C-dots) in Cicer arietinum L. Data represents mean ± SE of five experiments. Different letters indicate significant differences (P < 0.05). The results of corresponding one-way ANOVAs [P values, F ratios, degrees of freedom (df)] are shown beside each parameter.
The FTIR spectral characteristics of Cicer arietinum L. radicles subjected to arsenic (As) and/or carbon dot (C-dots).

head.ce:title: Carbon dot induces tolerance to arsenic by regulating arsenic uptake, reactive oxygen species detoxification and defense-related gene expression in Cicer arietinum L

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The scientific and technological applications of one of the nanomaterials viz.; carbon dot (C-dots), having extraordinary properties, is becoming an emerging and ongoing research area in recent times. In the present study, we have evaluated the effectiveness of C-dots in reducing arsenic (As) toxicity by analyzing physiological, biochemical and molecular parameters in Cicer arietinum L. The results revealed that As decreased the germination rate, growth, biomass, and membrane stability of the cell to a significant extent. Further, As was taken up by the growing seeds which eventually caused cell death. Levels of reactive oxygen species (ROS), stress markers (malondialdehyde), activities of defensive enzymes (glutathione-S-transferase and pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthetase) and non-enzymatic antioxidant contents (proline and glutathione) were increased under As stress. Moreover, As treatment resulted in the up-regulation of expressions of NADPH oxidase and defense-related genes in Cicer arietinum L. However, application of C-dots along with As improved the germination and growth of Cicer arietinum L. Exogenous application of C-dots, enhanced the expressions of defense-related genes and, contents of proline and glutathione, thereby causing considerable reductions in ROS, and malondialdehyde levels. Overall, this study suggests the possible involvement of C-dots in lowering the toxic effects of As on biomass by reducing As uptake and, inducing the activities/gene expressions and contents of enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidants.

ce:section.ce:para: Vibhuti Chandrakar Bhumika Yadu Jvoti Korram Manmohan L. Satnami Amit Duber Meetul Kumar S. Keshavkant.

Arsenic (As) is a non-essential metalloid, which instigates many toxic effects in the living systems (Kidwai et al., 2019). The plant roots absorb As predominantly in its inorganic forms: arsenate (AsV) and arsenite (AsIII). AsIII is considered to be more toxic to plants, since it permeates the membrane and reacts with the sulfhydryl groups of plant proteins and enzymes, disconcerting energy flow, causing leaking of electrolytes and generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) (Singh et al., 2015). A membrane localized enzyme NADPH oxidase (NOX) is also responsible for the production of ROS in plant cells (Reddy et al., 2015). These ROS oxidize/damage most major cellular bio-polymers such as lipid, protein, etc., resulting in the dysfunction, and sometimes death of the cells. A product of lipid peroxidation reaction; malondialdehyde (MDA) leads to disintegration of cellular organelles, oxidation and dysfunction of proteins and nucleic acids (Singh et al., 2015).

To counter the As stress, plants detoxify this metalloid by promptly converting it into AsIII, in the cytosol, by arsenate reductase. This AsIII is then expelled outside of the cell or sequestered into the vacuoles (Chandrakar et al., 2016). Under As stress, plants synthesize amino acids (proline) and non-protein thiol such as glutathione (GSH). This glutathione serves as a substrate for antioxidant enzyme; glutathione-S-transferase (GST) (Das and Roychoudhury, 2014). The non-enzymatic antioxidant amino acid (proline) is produced from the glutamate pathway catalyzed by pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthetase (P5CS), and the transcript level of this enzyme is elevated in a stressful condition (Chandrakar and Keshavkant, 2018a).

Nevertheless, plants show toxicity symptoms when the accumulation of As exceeds the threshold value, though, toxicity varies from plant to plant, soil type, As absorption, growth stage, etc. Therefore, study of As equilibrium and its accumulation in plant tissues is utmost essential to understanding its fate and to incapacitate its deleterious impacts (Chandrakar et al., 2018). In the last two decades, nanotechnology has emerged as an innovative as well as pronounced technique with widespread application against abiotic stresses (Praveen et al., 2018). One of the emerging nanomaterials; carbon dot (C-dots) is a fluorescent nanomaterial with size ranging between 2 and 5 nm in diameter (Li et al., 2020). These are graphene-built matters and serve as a two-dimensional building chunk for all the carbon-based materials (Chakravarty et al., 2015). Because of their exceptional properties, such as high surface-to-volume ratio, the ability to pierce the cells, high biochemical reactivity, better cell viability and swift distribution inside the cells, C-dots are used as plant growth regulators for improving biological properties. Recent reports reveal that nanomaterials offer better diffusion through the cuticle and have the ability to penetrate into plant cells due to their smaller diameters (Chakravarty et al., 2015).

Nanomaterials are known to reduce oxidative stress in plants, and can be considered as an important defense provisions against heavy metal/loid stress (Praveen et al., 2018). To date, there is inadequate information on systematic exploration about the ever-lifting concerns regarding the impact of nanomaterials on plants growing under abiotic stressed situations. Hence, due to the positive effects of C-dots in enhancing plant growth, it has been considered as an important tool in the present study for mitigating As toxicity in Cicer arietinum L. This species has been the focus of research due to it being frequently cultivated in As-contaminated region (Bhattacharya et al., 2012). The involvement of C-dots in As affected plants will reveal new insights on the favorable functions of C-dots as an ROS scavenger for the detoxification of As in plants. The effects of C-dots in response to As on germination percentage, radicle length, biomass and As contents, membrane stability index (MSI), cell death, ROS {superoxide (O2˙ˉ) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2)}, MDA content, antioxidants (proline and GSH), structural analysis by Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR), and gene expressions of NOX, GST and P5CS in Cicer arietinum L. were examined in the present study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of carbon dot
ce:section.ce:para: The C-dots were prepared by pyrolyzing citric acid (Dong et al., 2012). In a beaker, 2 g citric acid was put into PTFE autoclaves and heated to 180 °C for 3 h by hydrothermal method. This changed the color of liquid from colorless to pale yellow. After cooling the solution, 20 ml of base (0.25 M NaOH) was added and blue luminescent C-dots solution was obtained. Subsequent neutralization of the pH to 7.0 using NaOH resulted in the aqueous solution of C-dots (1.9 mM). Thereafter, the mixture was purified with a dialysis bag (MWCÕ3.0 kDa) for one day.

Characterization of the C-dots was done by recording the UV–Vis spectra using a spectrophotometer (Evolution 300, Thermo Scientific, USA) operated at a resolution of 20 nm. Fluorescence spectra were collected with a Varian (USA) Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (G9800AA) under excitation of 360 nm. The FTIR spectra were obtained on FTIR spectrophotometer (Nicolet iS10, Thermo Scientific, USA). Electron microscopic analysis was performed and high-resolution images were taken in a transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Tecnai TF-30, USA).

Seeds of Cicer arietinum L. (variety JG74) were surface sterilized using sodium hypochlorite solution (0.1%, v/v) for 5 min and then rinsed five times with MilliQ water (MW, Millipore, Gradient A-10, Burlington, MA). Surface sterilized seeds were transferred to sterile boxes (30 × 15 × 15 cm, 80 seeds box−1), lined with two sheets of filter papers, and were exposed to the following treatments: filter paper moistened with MW (control), 10 μM of As (sodium arsenite), 0.019 mM C-dots, and As + C-dots (10 μM + 0.019 mM) respectively. The boxes were kept in the dark for 6 day at 26±2 °C. The concentration of As (10 μM) selected in the present study significantly (47%) hampered the germination of Cicer arietinum L. seeds after two days of treatment. Further, C-dots at 0.019 mM concentration showed highest seed germination under stress condition, and so was selected. After every 24 h, growing seeds were supplied with their respective treatment solutions (10 ml each), and on sixth day, germination percentage, radicle length and biomass were assessed.

Content of As was determined by digesting 0.1 g of sample at 80 °C using the mixture of conc. HNO3, H2O2 and H2O (3/2/10, w/v/v) (Chandrakar and Keshavkant, 2018b). Quantification of As was done in an atomic absorption spectrometer coupled to a hydride generation system (Agilent, AA240, Santa Clara, CA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Membrane stability index and lipid peroxidation
ce:section.ce:para: For MSI determination, 10 radicles were dipped in 10 ml MW and then divided into two groups (Yadu et al., 2017a). One was heated at 40 °C for 30 min, and the other at 100 °C for 10 min, and electrical conductivity of the solution was measured as T1 and T2 respectively, using EC-TDS analyzer (CM-183, Elico, India). The MSI was calculated using the formula: MSI (%) = [1-T1/T2)] x100.

To determine MDA, 0.1 g of samples were mixed with 0.5% (w/v) 2-thiobarbituric acid in 20% (w/v) trichloroacetic acid (TCA). The content was boiled for 30 min, cooled and centrifuged at 11,000 rpm for 10 min (Velikova et al., 2000). Absorbance of the supernatant was recorded at 532 nm and non-specific absorbance was recorded at 600 nm. Content of MDA was estimated following extinction coefficient of 157 mM−1 cm−1 and expressed as nmol g−1 fresh mass (FM). Histochemical detection of lipid peroxidation was performed by staining the radicles with Schiff's reagent (Yadu et al., 2017a).

For this, radicles were incubated for 10 min in 15 ml Evans blue solution (0.025%, w/v, in 100 μM CaCl2, pH 5.6) (Yadu et al., 2017a). Thereafter, tissues were thoroughly rinsed with MW and imaged under a Binocular Digital CCD Compound Light Microscope (Labomed, India).

Evans blue dye was extracted by crushing 1 cm long tips of ten stained (Evans blue) radicles with sodium dodecyl sulfate (1 ml, 1%, w/v), followed by centrifugation at 14,000 rpm for 10 min. Absorbance of the supernatants was measured at 600 nm using a spectrophotometer (Lambda-25, PerkinElmer, USA), and expressed as μg g−1 FM (Yadu et al., 2017b).

For O2˙ˉ content, 0.2 g radicles were incubated for 6 h in phosphate buffer (20 mM, pH 6.0), consisting of XTT (500 μM), in the dark at 26 °C, on a shaker at 50 rpm. Absorbance of the solution was read at 470 nm (Schopfer et al., 2001), content of O2˙ˉ was calculated using extinction coefficient 2.16 × 104 M−1 cm−1 and referred as μmol g−1 FM.

For H2O2 content, 0.2 g radicles were extracted with 0.1% (w/v) TCA and centrifuged (11,000 rpm, 15 min) at 25 °C (Velikova et al., 2000). Supernatant was mixed with equal volumes of phosphate buffer (10 mM, pH 7) and potassium iodide (1 M), and absorbance was read at 390 nm H2O2 content was calculated using extinction coefficient 0.28 μM−1 cm−1 and expressed as μmol g−1 FM.
Histochemical localizations of O2˙ˉ and H2O2 were done by nitroblue tetrazolium and 3,3-diaminobenzidine staining, respectively (Rughani et al., 2016).
Productions of O2˙ˉ and H2O2 were localized respectively using dihydroethidium and 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate, and imaged using a Confocal microscope DM 1000 LED (Leica, Germany).

For this, 0.5 g of radicles was homogenized with 10 ml of 3% (w/v) sulpho-salicylic acid and centrifuged (5000 rpm, 15 min). Supernatant (2 ml) was mixed with equal volumes of acid ninhydrin reagent and glacial acetic acid (Bates et al., 1973). The mixture was heated at 100 °C for 1 h in a boiling water-bath and cooled at 4 °C for 10 min. Thereafter, 4 ml of toluene was added to it and absorbance of the end product was read at 520 nm. Concentration of proline was determined using its standard curve and expressed as mg g−1 FM.

To estimate total GSH, 0.2 g samples were mixed with 1.5 ml of chilled sulfo-salicylic acid (5%, w/v) and then centrifuged (14,000 rpm, 10 min, 4 °C) (Griffith, 1980). The reaction mixture was prepared by adding 20 μl of supernatant, 200 μl of potassium phosphate buffer (100 mM, pH 7.5), 560 μl of ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (10 mM), and 100 μl of 5,5′-dithio-bis-2-nitrobenzoic acid [6 mM, dissolved in potassium phosphate buffer of 100 mM (pH 7.5), and 100 μl of NADPH (2.1 mM)]. The reaction was initiated by adding 1 unit of glutathione reductase and the increase in absorbance was monitored at 412 nm. Content was calculated using extinction coefficient 13.6 × 103 M−1 cm−1 and expressed as μmol g−1 FM.

For this, 5 g radicles were extracted with 1 ml of 50 mM phosphate buffer (pH 6.8) containing sucrose (250 mM), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (3 mM), dithiothreitol (1.25 mM) and phenylmethylsulfonyl fluoride (PMSF, 1 mM), followed by centrifugation (10,000 rpm, 10 min) (Hejl and Koster, 2004). The upper layer was again spun (25,000 rpm, 45 min) and resuspended in 50 μl phosphate buffer (50 mM, pH 6.8) containing sucrose (250 mM), Triton X-100 (0.5%, v/v) and, 1 mM each of dithiothreitol and PMSF. The mixture was then incubated on an ice-bath for 1 h and centrifuged at 25,000 rpm for 45 min. The supernatant (50 μl) was mixed with 950 μl of 50 mM tris-HCl (pH 7.5) containing 0.5 mM 2,3-bis-(2-methoxy-4-nitro-5-sulfophenyl)-2h-tetrazolium-5-carboxanilide and 100 μM NADPH, and the change in absorbance was measured at 470 nm. Rate of O2˙ˉ generation was calculated using an extinction coefficient of 2.16 × 104 M−1 cm−1 and expressed as mol O2˙ˉ min−1 g−1 FM.

Activity of GST was determined using the assay mixture comprising of 0.5 mM 1-chloro-2,4-dinitrobenzene, 1 mM GSH and 100 mM phosphate buffer (pH 6.5), followed by incubation for 10 min at laboratory temperature (Ricci et al., 1984). The rate of increase in absorbance was determined at 340 nm for 10 min by adding 10 μl GSH and 50 μl enzyme extract. The enzymatic activity was calculated using an extinction coefficient of 9.6 mM−1 cm−1.

To determine P5CS activity, 0.2 g of radicles were macerated with tris-HCl buffer (2 ml, 100 mM, pH 7.4) comprising of 2-mercaptoethanol (100 mM), magnesium chloride (10 mM) and PMSF (1 mM), followed by centrifugation at 11,000 rpm for 15 min (Singh et al., 2015). Reaction assay (3 ml) was prepared by adding tris-HCl (100 mM, pH 7.2), sodium glutamate (75 mM), magnesium chloride (25 mM), ATP (5 mM) and supernatant (200 μl). To initiate the reaction, NADPH (0.4 mM) was added and the absorbance was taken at 340 nm.

Total RNA was extracted with simultaneous removal of genomic DNA following the hot phenol method. Using a Nanodrop Spectrophotometer (ND1000, Thermo Scientific, USA), the quality and concentration of RNA were measured. First strand of cDNA was synthesized by using 1 μg of total RNA (cDNA synthesis kit, Invitrogen, USA). Beta actin was used as a control. Specific primers for NOX (sense-5′-TGGCAATAGCANTTGGTGTTG-3’; antisense-5′-GAGANACAGCAGCACAATTGAC-3′), GST (sense-5′-ATCACTAGAAATTACAAGGG-3'; antisense-5′-TTCCGTTCGTTGAAAGATTC-3′) and P5CS (sense-5′-CTTCATTGGCAATCCGAAGT-3’; antisense-5′-TCCCTTGAGGTCACAAGTCC-3′) were designed using primer 3 (http://frodo.wi.mit.edu) (Yadu et al., 2018). The PCR was set at 95 °C for 5 min (initial denaturation), 35 cycles at 95 °C for 60 s, 52-58 °C for 45 s, 72 °C for 60 s, then 10 min extension at 72 °C. Electrophoresis was done in 1.5% (w/v) agarose gel using 1x TAE buffer (50 V, for 40 min). Gene expression was analyzed and concluded by comparing the intensities of bands under a Gel-Doc system (BioRad, USA).

This technique was used to detect the structural changes and probable binding sites induced by the presence of As and C-dots in Cicer arietinum L. For this, the radicles were ground into fine powder by using liquid nitrogen and were filled in the micro cup of 2 mm internal diameter (D’Souza et al., 2008). The absorption spectrum was recorded at laboratory temperature using FTIR spectrometer in the region 500-4000 cm−1. Each spectrum is displayed in terms of transmittance as calculated from the reflectance-absorbance spectrum.

All the experiments were repeated twice with five replicates. Analysis of variance was done to reveal the effects of C-dots and As on studied variables. Data were analyzed for Duncan's multiple range tests, at P < 0.05, and are mean ± SE of five separate observations. Statistical analyses were performed using SPSS software (Ver 16.0).

Sharp and visible range absorption bands at 367 nm were found in the UV–Vis spectra of C-dots, corresponding to absorption of an aromatic π system. The C-dots showed fluorescence emission band at 490 nm when was excited at 380 nm (Fig. 1 A). The TEM image indicated that the synthesized C-dots were mostly spherical and well dispersed with their size ranging between 3.35 and 5 nm (Fig. 1B). It was corresponding to the (002) planes of graphitic carbon, indicating the graphitic nature of C-dots. The FTIR spectra (Fig. 1C) showed that C-dots possessed broad absorption bands of O–H and N–H stretching vibrations of amine groups (3425 cm−1) and –COOH (1627 cm−1), indicating the existence of NH bending and aromatic ring structures on the surface of C-dots. Additionally, the C-dots showed absorption of stretching vibrations of C–H at 2950 cm−1 as well as below 1350 cm-1, suggesting that the C-dots contained some incompletely carbonized citric acid. It should be noted that no characteristic absorption bands of aromatic compounds (stretching vibration of C–H in aromatic rings around 3000-3100 cm−1, very thin vibration of aromatic rings around 1450-1650 cm−1) were observed in the FTIR spectra, suggesting the absence of aromatic compounds in the obtained C-dots.

The natural and anthropogenic disposals of As increases its concentration to a damaging point in both soil and water. Plants growing in such environment develop toxicity symptoms by absorption/uptake of As which is further passed to the food chain, thereby creating issues of global concern (Kidwai et al., 2019). Preliminary analyses to observe the effects of As and its combination with C-dots began with evaluating the physiological growth indicators such as germination percentage, radicle length, and biomass accrual which was measured in terms of DM (dry mass). In our study, As exhibited toxicity to Cicer arietinum L. and showed severe damaging effects on the growth indicators (Table 1 ). Analyses revealed that exposure of As to Cicer arietinum L. caused significant (up to 57%) decrease in germination percentage, radicle length and DM accumulation, indicative of a negative relationship between As and growth traits (Table 1). These effects might be the outcome of (i) over accumulation of As in the radicles, (ii) reduced absorption of nutrients by the roots, (iii) alterations in cell wall components, (iv) decrease in membrane stability, (v) insufficient availability of energy, and (vi) over production of ROS harmful for cellular biomolecules and other important metabolites, which play crucial roles in growth and development of plants (Singh et al., 2015). On the other hand, co-exposure of As with C-dots improved the germination percentage, growth and DM, which demonstrated the role of C-dots in promoting (up to 66%) plant growth even in the presence of As. Chakravarty et al. (2015) also reported that, the application of C-dots (0.2 mg ml−1) substantially increases the growth rates of Coriandrum sativam L. and Allium sativum plants, and suggested that C-dots induced an increase in the level of certain proteins that are essential for normal plant development.

The results indicated that As gets accumulated (0.15 μg As g−1 DM) in As challenged Cicer arietinum L. radicles (Table 1), and the results obtained are in agreement with the report studied in Solanum melongena L. (treated with 25 μM As) by Singh et al. (2015). Being the primary armaments of plants, roots accrue good amount of metalloid and show toxicity symptoms. A negative correlation of As accrual with radicle length (R2 = 0.95), and MSI (R2 = 0.85) was observed (Fig. 3 ). However, supplementation of C-dots limited the uptake of As (0.10 μg As g−1 DM) by the root cells, and hence lowered (33%) its accumulation. This could be correlated with the key roles of C-dots in reducing ROS, improving membrane integrity and enhancing antioxidants, and may be regarded as one of the mechanisms for lowering As-toxicity in Cicer arietinum L. (Chandrakar et al., 2018).

The physiological status of Cicer arietinum L. under As stress was also analyzed by measuring the cellular concentration of ROS. In this study, As induced severe oxidative stress as indicated by the over accumulation of ROS (O2˙ˉ: 600% and H2O2: 4643%), compared to control radicles (Table 1). This may be attributed as a result of affinity of As for sulfhydryl groups of proteins and enzymes causing inactivation of key enzymes implicated in the scavenging of ROS (Singh et al., 2015). Similarly, Farooq et al. (2018) has also reported As-mediated increase in ROS content in Brassica napus L. exposed with 25 and 100 μM As. In the present study, accumulation of ROS is positively correlated with MDA (R2 = 0.95 and R2 = 0.89), which could be a sign of As-induced oxidative injuries, damage of membrane lipids through peroxidation reaction and ultimately death of the cells (R2 = 0.76 and R2 = 0.98). Taking together the results of the present study, alleviation of As-toxicity by C-dots may be related with the lower amounts of ROS (100–2936%) in Cicer arietinum L. and higher antioxidant enzyme (GST) activity, which resulted in improved growth of the plants under As stress. These results were further verified by histochemical staining of O2˙ˉ, H2O2, lipid peroxidation and by studying cell death (Fig. 4 a,b,c).

To identify the production sites of O2˙ˉ and H2O2, radicles were colored with specific stain solutions. Staining revealed that formation of both blue formazan and brownish reaction products was more in As treated radicles, than those in controls (Fig. 4b and c). However, C-dots application into As resulted in less abundance of colored patches/O2˙ˉ and H2O2 production.

Furthermore, in-vivo visualization of O2 •− and H2O2 production was done using different fluorescent dyes. Resulting images were evaluated for the presence of ROS, which revealed that intensity of emission increased in As treated radicles. Signal of ROS in the control was visible only in the stele region. Visualization of O2 •− showed sharp increase upon exposure to As in the radicles of Cicer arietinum L., thereby verifying biochemical results. In the As treated radicles, signal of ROS occurred not only in stele region (more intense than that of control), but also in exodermis, endodermis and parenchyma cells of cortex region. While, production of O2 •− was less in the control and C-dots treated radicles (Fig. 5 a), maximum production of O2 •− and H2O2 was observed in As treated radicles. Application of C-dots decreased its production, as evidenced by low fluorescence emission (Fig. 5b).

Membrane lipids are more susceptible to ROS attack, and their alteration is a reliable indication of oxidative stress in plants. In the present study, significant increase in lipid peroxidation with decreased MSI after As treatment in Cicer arietinum L. was evidenced by increased MDA content. This phenomenon also has been observed in Arabidopsis thaliana when treated with 10 and 25 μM As (Kidwai et al., 2019). Our study showed that addition of C-dots decreased the MDA level in As treated Cicer arietinum L. which was accompanied by the increased MSI, hence proving to be a positive growth regulator. In the presence of As, MSI was 51%, which was improved by 68% when C-dots were applied along with As solution. Damage to membrane integrity was also detected by the uptake of a non-permeable dye (Evans blue) into the radicles of Cicer arietinum L. treated with As and/or C-dots. This stain could penetrate through distorted membranes and then stain cells. The As treated tissues had deeper blue staining as compared to control, indicating loss of membrane integrity (Fig. 4a). Absorption of Evans blue increased by 153% when radicles were exposed to As which is significantly higher than that detected in control (Table 1). The radicles subjected to C-dots along with As showed faintly stained tissues and less (23%) uptake of Evans blue. Previous finding also reported the increment in absorption of Evans blue under salt stress (Yadu et al., 2017b).

In the As treated radicles, level of MDA was found to be increased considerably (66%), than the MW grown control. On the other side, in C-dots amended As grown samples, significantly lower levels (18%) of MDA was measured in Cicer arietinum L. (Table 1).

The Schiff's reagent staining revealed intense pink coloration/higher magnitude of MDA formation in the As exposed radicles, as compared to control. In contrast, addition of C-dots into As solution revealed remarkably lower lipid peroxidation reaction, expressed by faint stain (Fig. 4d).

Non-enzymatic antioxidants; proline and GSH accumulate in plants upon abiotic stresses (Xalxo et al., 2017). Upon As exposure, an increase of 66% in the proline content and by 148% in that of GSH were recorded in comparison to the control; however, C-dots supplementation to As challenged Cicer arietinum L. further enhanced (95% and 198% respectively) their levels (Table 1). Under As stress, proteins get collapsed into amino acids and further, converted into proline for storage. Stress-induced perturbations in the electron transport system led to accrual of NADH and H+; hence accumulation of proline might be an adaptive mechanism for plummeting the level of accumulated NADH and H+. Accumulation of proline under exposure of 25 μM As has been reported by Vezza et al. (2018). It has also been reported that proline quenches free radicals directly and/or by stimulating activities of defensive enzymes, thereby stabilizing proteins and membranes, while GSH is involved in the synthesis of phytochelatins which help in the sequestration of As inside the cells (Saha et al., 2017). Also, GSH is the vital source of non-protein thiol in plants, and plays a key role in protecting the cells against oxidative damage by maintaining redox status. Higher proline and GSH lowered the oxidative damage in plants, which is consistent with earlier reports (Saha et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Activities and gene expressions of NADPH oxidase, glutathione-S-transferase and pyrroline-5-carboxylate synthetase
ce:section.ce:para: Exposure to As resulted in enhanced activities of NOX (546%), GST (93%) and P5CS (147%) as compared to control (Fig. 6 a–c). On the other hand, addition of C-dots into As solution diminished the NOX activity by 41%. However, activities of GST and P5CS were observed to further increase by 145% and 302% respectively in Cicer arietinum L. radicles, when C-dots were exogenously added into As solution.

Expression analyses of NOX, GST and P5CS gene unveiled that these genes had increased transcription levels in response to As in the radicles of Cicer arietinum L., compared to control (Fig. 6d). The application of C-dots with As strongly repressed the accumulation of NOX transcripts and, up-regulated the expression levels of GST and P5CS, which were in agreement with their biochemical analyses.

In plants, production of O2˙ˉ has been catalyzed by a plasma membrane bound enzyme, NOX by transferring electrons from NADPH to molecular oxygen in the apoplastic space. Previously, it has been reported that under abiotic stresses, NOX activity is increased that leads to production of O2˙ˉ (Das and Kar, 2016). In our study as well, a positive correlation (R2 = 0.86) was found between NOX activity (546%) and O2˙ˉ (600%) (Fig. 3). Application of C-dots reduced the NOX activity and thus, lowered the production of O2˙ˉ. In this way C-dots exhibited a protective function against As-induced oxidative stress in Cicer arietinum L.

Activities of GST and P5CS increased upon exposure to As and were further enhanced when C-dots was applied along with As, indicating that these enzymes help in the detoxification of ROS. The results of our study clearly indicate significant increments in the GST and P5CS activities (total activity and gene expression) in As-stressed Cicer arietinum L. Up-regulation of GST and P5CS genes in the presence of C-dots suggests the involvement of these enzymes in facilitating tolerance against oxidative stress, which signifies through the improved growth of the plant. Similarly, increase in the activities of these two enzymes has been reported earlier in Cajanus cajan L. treated with 75 ppm fluoride (Yadu et al., 2018). It can be suggested that alterations in the activities of these enzymes was due to the modified expression of the genes controlling the biosynthesis of these enzymes.

To summarize, As treatment resulted in decreased germination, growth, biomass, MSI, enhanced As and Evans blue accumulation, oxidative injury, up-regulated NOX and antioxidative defense responses, and increased proline and GSH contents in the tested radicles. Application of C-dots to As exposed Cicer arietinum L. was supportive towards activation/up-regulation of defensive enzymes, enhancement of antioxidants, and diminution in As uptake. Our findings conclude positive roles of C-dots in the amelioration of As toxicity in Cicer arietinum L. suggesting a novel approach to improve plant's tolerance to metal/loid toxicity. Further research is required to interpret the underlying molecular mechanism(s) of increased plant tolerance in response to C-dots under As stress.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Department of Science & Technology, New Delhi (DST/INSPIRE Fellowship/2013/791, dated January 23, 2013), Defense Research & Development Organization, New Delhi (DRDE/TC/05414/Proj/TASK-220/16 Dated June 27, 2016) and Chhattisgarh Council of Science and Technology, Raipur (2741/CCOST/MRP/2015, dated March 24, 2015). Authors are also grateful to National Centre for Natural Resources and School of Life Sciences, Pt. Ravishankar Shukla University, Raipur, for providing FTIR and confocal microscope facility.
